context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on the SWOT analysis for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole assignment from the beginning in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you get unstuck! Before I re-explain everything, let's figure out where the confusion actually starts.

You've done previous assignments leading up to this one - a Spider Diagram, a Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors. Do you have those completed? And which city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student says they are lost and wants a full explanation. However, giving them a complete walkthrough would not serve the Socratic method or guided discovery principles. Instead, I should acknowledge their confusion, give a very brief orientation, and then ask a targeted question to find out where exactly they are stuck and get them moving. I should not re-explain the entire assignment - that's the instructor's job and it's already in the assignment description. I want to find out what they already know and build from there.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I did them, but now it all kind of blurs together. My city is Jakarta, and I’m not sure how those older assignments are supposed to turn into the SWOT table.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, Jakarta is a rich case study for climate action! Let's build that bridge.

Think about your Spider Diagram - it showed how Jakarta is performing across the 6 urban pathways. In your own words, what does a 'weakness' in a pathway on that diagram look like? What would that tell you about Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has confirmed they completed prior assignments and identified Jakarta as their city. The confusion is about how to connect previous work to the SWOT table. Rather than explaining the connection for them, I should ask a targeted Socratic question that helps them see the link themselves. I'll start with the most fundamental building block: what the SWOT categories mean in this specific context, since that's the bridge between their prior work and the table. I'll keep it bite-sized and ask just one question.

exchanges.student: I guess it means Jakarta isn’t doing well in that pathway, but I’m not really sure what counts as “doing well” versus just having some policies on paper. That’s where I get stuck, so I kind of need the whole thing broken down more simply.
exchanges.tutor:
That instinct you have about 'policies on paper' is actually really sharp - hold onto it.

Think about Jakarta specifically: can you name one climate-related challenge the city faces that persists *despite* any policies that might exist on paper? What does that gap tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a genuinely good conceptual question - the difference between performative policy and actual performance. This is worth engaging with briefly via a Socratic question rather than explaining it. I should not break down the whole assignment as they request. Instead, I'll use their own intuition to push them toward an answer. Their instinct about 'policies on paper' is actually quite sharp and I should build on it.

exchanges.student: Maybe flooding? But then I start wondering if that’s a stressor or a threat or a weakness or all of them, and then I get confused about the categories again. Could you maybe just walk me through what each part of SWOT means in this assignment?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but I think you're closer than you realize - let's work it out with your flooding example rather than starting over.

Here's the key question: is flooding something *Jakarta itself* causes or fails to prevent (internal), or is it something that happens *to* Jakarta from outside forces (external)? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same confusion about SWOT categories. This is the second or third time they've asked for a full explanation rather than engaging with a specific question. I should be firm about not re-explaining everything, but I can help them untangle their specific example of flooding using Socratic questions. Their confusion between stressor/threat/weakness is actually a productive one that they can resolve themselves by thinking about the internal vs. external distinction, which is the core logic of SWOT. I'll point them toward that distinction with one focused question.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly what keeps tripping me up though, because it feels like both. Flooding happens to Jakarta, but also the city’s infrastructure and planning seem part of the problem, so I end up feeling like every example could go in every box.
exchanges.tutor:
That tension you're feeling is actually good thinking - many factors do have multiple dimensions. Here's a simple reframe: ask yourself what Jakarta *controls* versus what it *doesn't control*.

Now apply that to flooding: what part of Jakarta's flooding problem does the city have direct control over, and what part is outside its control?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing genuine frustration and circling on the same conceptual issue for several exchanges. This is a good moment to be a bit less Socratic and use a concrete reframe to help them separate the two dimensions, without giving the answer. The internal/external distinction can be clarified by asking them to think about what Jakarta *controls* versus what it *doesn't control*. I'll give them a concrete reframe as a hint and then ask them to apply it to flooding themselves.

exchanges.student: I can kind of see that, but I’m still pretty overwhelmed and worried I’m organizing it wrong. Could you just give me a really simple from-scratch way to sort things into strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor:
I think we've talked through enough of the concepts together - continuing to discuss categories in the abstract isn't going to unstick you.

Here's what I want you to do: take flooding as your example and write one sentence placing it somewhere in the SWOT table for one pathway. Don't worry about being perfect. Just try it and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked multiple times for a full explanation of SWOT categories. This is becoming a redundant pattern - 4-5 exchanges circling the same issue. Per the rules, I should be less Socratic here given the frustration, but I still cannot give the answer directly. More importantly, I should redirect them to actually attempt something written, since we've talked enough conceptually. I should be direct about this and push them to try applying what we've discussed to Jakarta, even imperfectly. I'll acknowledge their anxiety but firmly redirect.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe: “A weakness is that Jakarta’s drainage and infrastructure are not strong enough to handle flooding.” I’m still not sure if that’s too vague though, and I kind of feel like I need the bigger picture too.
exchanges.tutor:
Good - that's a legitimate weakness and you placed it correctly. But the assignment asks for specific, concrete examples. 

Can you add one concrete detail to that sentence - a specific neighborhood, statistic, or known infrastructure gap in Jakarta that backs it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine attempt and produced a decent first sentence. I should acknowledge this positively but push them to make it more specific and concrete, as the assignment requires '2-3 specific, concrete examples' with 'relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible.' I should not validate vagueness but help them see how to sharpen it. I should also not get drawn into re-explaining the bigger picture again - that's been a recurring deflection. I'll give brief positive feedback and ask one targeted question to help them add specificity.

exchanges.student: I don’t know the details off the top of my head, and that’s why I’m panicking a bit. Can you just explain the overall structure one more time in a super simple way, because I feel lost again?
exchanges.tutor:
I don't think re-explaining the structure is what you actually need right now. What you need is your research.

Go back to your Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors for Jakarta. What specific facts or findings did you record there? Start there - the SWOT table is meant to synthesize *that* work.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is cycling back to asking for a full explanation again - this is now a very clear redundant pattern across multiple exchanges. Per the rules, I should not re-explain the structure. The student's real issue here seems to be a lack of research/source material rather than conceptual confusion. I should be direct and honest about this: the reason they feel stuck is that they need to go back to their previous assignments and do some research on Jakarta. I should redirect them firmly but supportively toward their sources.

exchanges.student: I know, I just can’t see how to turn those notes into the table. It all feels like separate pieces, and I’m confused about basically all of it again.
exchanges.tutor:
I want to be honest with you: we've been talking in circles, and that's not helping you move forward.

Here's what I need you to do before our next exchange: open your Spider Diagram and tell me which **one pathway** scored lowest for Jakarta. Just that. Then we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and going in circles. We've now had many exchanges on the same general confusion without the student making meaningful forward progress. Per the rules, when a student is going in circles, it's ok to be less Socratic and use relevant examples - but I still cannot give the answer. I think the most productive thing now is to give a very concrete, direct prompt that forces the student to engage with their actual prior work rather than talking abstractly. I should also be honest that continuing to discuss this abstractly is not helping them. I'll be direct and firm.

exchanges.student: I think maybe governance/civic engagement was one of the lower ones, but I’m not even sure. Could you maybe just give me a simple example of how one pathway turns into SWOT, because I can’t picture the conversion at all?
exchanges.tutor:
Good - governance/civic engagement is a solid starting point. Now let's stay there.

From what you know about Jakarta - even generally - what is one reason why civic engagement around climate action might be weak there? Think about who makes decisions in the city and who gets left out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made some progress by identifying governance/civic engagement as a weak pathway - I should acknowledge this. However, they are again asking me to do the conversion for them by providing an example. I cannot do this as it would essentially give them part of the answer. However, I can use what they've just said to push them forward with a very targeted Socratic question about Jakarta's governance specifically. We've been going in circles long enough - I need to get them to commit to something concrete about Jakarta's governance weaknesses rather than continuing abstractly.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, when the student says they don't know specific details and are panicking, the tutor immediately redirects to 'go back to your research' without first diagnosing whether the student's confusion is about research retrieval, synthesis skills, or something else. Similarly in Turn 9, when the student says they can't see how to turn notes into the table, the tutor acknowledges the circular pattern but still doesn't ask a targeted diagnostic question to identify the specific breakdown point before responding.

The tutor cycles through similar Socratic prompts across Turns 3-6 without meaningfully advancing the student's understanding. The student repeatedly expresses the same confusion about SWOT categories, and the tutor keeps asking variations of the same 'internal vs. external' framing question without consolidating, reframing more concretely, or switching strategy. By Turn 6, the student is still stuck in the same place as Turn 3, and the tutor only shifts approach in Turn 6 by asking for a written attempt.

grade.overview: The tutor does well overall: maintains a Socratic approach throughout, never gives away answers or does the student's work, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive tone. The main weaknesses are a prolonged spiral in the middle turns where the same internal/external framing is repeated without sufficient strategy variation, and occasional missed opportunities to diagnose the specific nature of the student's confusion before redirecting.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor consistently refuses to provide direct answers, instead using questions to guide the student toward their own reasoning, and never produces submission-ready content. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turns 8-9, when the student expresses panic and confusion about synthesis, the tutor redirects to prior work without first asking a targeted diagnostic question to identify exactly where the breakdown is occurring. 2.1 loses all 4 points because Turns 3 through 6 show the tutor cycling through essentially the same internal/external distinction question in different phrasings while the student remains stuck in the same confusion, without consolidating, simplifying, or meaningfully reframing the approach until Turn 6 when the tutor finally asks for a written attempt. Communication quality and assignment anchoring are both strong throughout the conversation.
